coredata.dc:title: Transient photovoltage study of the kinetics and synergy of electron/hole co-extraction in MoS2/Ag-In-Zn-S/carbon dot photocatalysts for promoted hydrogen production

coredata.dc:description: Narrow-bandgap semiconductors play a key role in visible-light photocatalysis for solar energy conversion, where the severe charge trapping and recombination have been the main issues hindering the activity improvement. Here, MoS2 nanosheets and ferrocene-functionalized carbon dots (fCDs) were loaded on Ag-In-Zn-S quantum dots (AIZS QDs) to form ternary composite photocatalysts towards efficient electron/hole co-extraction and profound kinetics understanding of the related processes. The optimal hydrogen production rate of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs reached 8.52 mmol g−1h−1, which is dramatically higher than AIZS QDs (17.67 times), indicating the synergistic effect of electron/hole co-extraction compared to AIZS/fCDs (4.67 times) and AIZS/MoS2 (6.16 times). Transient photovoltage spectroscopy tests and the apparent kinetics analysis were carried out in terms of hydrogen production rate vs. effective surface charge carriers in the cases of pure QDs, hole extraction (binary AIZS/fCDs) and electron/hole coextraction, which indicated the hydrogen evolution reaction on the composites proceeds via the electrocatalysis based Volmer-Tafel process, involving the combination of two H* atoms to H2.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Narrow-bandgap semiconductors play a key role in visible-light photocatalysis for solar energy conversion, where the severe charge trapping and recombination have been the main issues hindering the activity improvement. Here, MoS2 nanosheets and ferrocene-functionalized carbon dots (fCDs) were loaded on Ag-In-Zn-S quantum dots (AIZS QDs) to form ternary composite photocatalysts towards efficient electron/hole co-extraction and profound kinetics understanding of the related processes. The optimal hydrogen production rate of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs reached 8.52 mmol g−1h−1, which is dramatically higher than AIZS QDs (17.67 times), indicating the synergistic effect of electron/hole co-extraction compared to AIZS/fCDs (4.67 times) and AIZS/MoS2 (6.16 times). Transient photovoltage spectroscopy tests and the apparent kinetics analysis were carried out in terms of hydrogen production rate vs. effective surface charge carriers in the cases of pure QDs, hole extraction (binary AIZS/fCDs) and electron/hole coextraction, which indicated the hydrogen evolution reaction on the composites proceeds via the electrocatalysis based Volmer-Tafel process, involving the combination of two H* atoms to H2.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic hydrogen production plays a vital role in the fossil fuel crisis and environmental pollution [1–4]. For efficient solar energy conversion, lots of materials have been developed for visible active photocatalysts, especially narrow band gap semiconductors [5–8]. However, the inherent shortcomings of these materials, such as poor charge separation, severe charge recombination and subsequent photo-corrosion, have limited the further development of photocatalytic hydrogen production [9–12]. It has been more and more realized that the unexpected hole trapping is the one of the biggest challenges in charge recombination and photo-corrosion due to the relatively slow mobility of the holes. Lots of recent works have been reported on the strategies to improve the hole extraction, including both charge separation efficiency and oxidizing reactions [13–15]. Moreover, strategies for simultaneous electron/hole extraction have been developed by space-separated modification of both cocatalysts [16–22], such as CoOX/TiO2/Pt [23] and Co(II)/P-doped g-C3N4/Pt [24].

For synergistic electron/hole extraction, related dynamic and mechanism studies play a crucial role on catalyst design and activity boosting. The charge carrier kinetics at the catalyst surface and interface has attracted lots of research interest in the photocatalytic system, for which several methods have been employed for the kinetics study, such as transient absorption spectroscopy and transient photovoltage spectroscopy [25–27]. However, it still lacks the profound understanding and correlation of the charge carrier kinetics and the actual photocatalytic reaction kinetics [28–31].

In this work, an efficient ternary photocatalyst was built by introducing MoS2 as the electron acceptor, and active sites and functionalized carbon dots (fCDs) as the hole acceptor onto Ag-In-Zn-S (AIZS) QDs focusing on the investigation of the kinetics and synergetic effect of electron/hole extraction. We first combined ferrocene formic acid (FcA) with CDs to obtain fCDs with strong hole accepting capability [32], which were then used together with MoS2 nanosheets to co-modify AIZS QDs for synergistic electron/hole extraction. The hydrogen production rate of ternary MoS2/AIZS/fCDs reaches 8.52 mmol g−1h−1, which is 17.67 times higher than that of AIZS QDs and much superior to the binary AIZS/fCDs (4.6 times) and AIZS/MoS2 (6.16 times) composites. TPV was used to investigate the electron/hole extraction and recombination kinetics, focusing on the interaction and synergy of different strategies. More importantly, further detailed analysis of the apparent reaction kinetics indicates, for the first time, that the hydrogen evolution reaction on the composites proceeds via the kinetically more favorable Volmer-Tafel process in electrocatalysis. This work provides an important fact for the design of photocatalysts with efficient charge separation through synergistic electron/hole extraction.
Preparation of CDs. The CDs were obtained by electrochemical-etching of graphite rods in pure water [33].

Preparation of fCDs. The synthesis process of fCDs is similar to our previous work [32]. 0.5 mL of ferrocenecarboxylic acid (FcA) in dimethyl sulfoxide solution (1 mg/mL) and 10 mL of CDs aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) were mixed to form a uniform solution, which was put in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 140 °C for 4 h.

Preparation of MoS2 nanosheets. The synthesis process of MoS2 nanosheets is according to the literature with modifications [34]. 1 mmol of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and 14 mmol of CH4N2S were added into 35 mL of deionized water, which was then put into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and kept at 220 °C for 18 h. The obtained material was rinsed with 0.1 m HCl for 2 h to remove NH4+ completely, washed with water and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C in vacuum.

Preparation of AIZS QDs. The synthesis process is same as our previous work [35]. Simply, 0.17 mmol of silver nitrate, 1.7 mmol of indium nitrate, and 0.85 mmol of zinc acetate were added in 5.5 mL distilled water to form a clear solution, where mmol of L-cysteine in mL water was added. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 8.5 with 0.1 M NaOH solution. Thioacetamide solution (4 mL of 3.25 M) was added in the above solution, which was then put into an autoclave and kept at 110 °C for 4 h. Finally, the AIZS QDs were centrifuged and washed three times by water and ethanol.

Preparation of AIZS/fCDs. The synthesis process is same as the literature[32]. The simple steps are as follows: silver nitrate, indium nitrate, L-cysteine and zinc acetate were added in 5.5 mL distilled water and added solution of fCDs when the pH of the mixed solution turned to 8.5. Thioacetamide solution was added in above solution, which was then react at 110 °C for 4 h. Finally, AIZS/fCDs was rinsed three times by water and ethyl alcohol.

Preparation of AIZS/MoS2. 11 mg of MoS2 nanosheets were added into 100 mg of AIZS QDs solution and stirred overnight to prepare AIZS/MoS2. The mass fractions MoS2 to AIZS were turned from 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%, 11% to 13%, respectively.

Preparation of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs. 11 mg of MoS2 nanosheets were added into 100 mg AIZS/fCDs dispersion and mechanically stirred for 12 h, like that of AIZS/MoS2. The mass fractions MoS2 to AIZS/fCDs were turned from 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%, 11% to 13%, respectively.

Concentration determination of the samples: 200 μL of the sample solution was added to ethanol for centrifugation to form precipitation and dried in a vacuum oven for one night to obtain the weight of sample. The approximate concentration is about 28–35 mg/mL.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a PIXcel3D diffractometer (Empyrean, Holland Panalytical) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). An FEI-Tecnai F20 electron microscope was used for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) with an accelerating voltage 200 kV. Valence states of the elements were obtained on a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photo-electron spectrometer (XPS) with Al Kα radiation (hυ = 1283.3 eV). UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on Cary 8450 and Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometers, respectively. Time-resolved PL (TRPL) for the samples was measured in the range of 350–900 nm by using a NKT PIL1-040-40FC photonics spectrometer with the excitation of 405 nm. Photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were acquired on a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (CHI Instruments, Inc., China), where the working electrode was made by spraying the samples on FTO glass. TPV spectroscopy was employed for the interface kinetics study by using a self-made system in our lab, excited by nanosecond laser pulses (355 nm, 5 Hz) from a third harmonic Nd:YAG laser (Beamtech Optonics Co., Ltd).

A multi-channel photocatalytic reactor with white LED light source (PCX-50B Discover, Beijing Perfectlight, China) was used for photocatalytic performance test. The reaction solution was formed by dissolving fresh photocatalysts (20 mg) in 15 mL aqueous solution of 0.25 M ascorbic acid as the sacrificial agent, which was transferred to a 40 mL reaction via land bubbled with nitrogen for 15 min. The whole reaction process was continuously illuminated for 5 h with visible light from LEDs (power intensity of 100 mW cm−2). Finally, the hydrogen production rate was estimated by extracting 1.5 mL of the evolved gas every 1 h and quickly injecting it into a gas chromatograph (GC7900, TCD detector) to detect the amount of hydrogen produced.

The size and morphology of the QDs and composites were first characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a and 1b, the AIZS QDs and CDs are well dispersed with no obvious agglomeration. Both the AIZS QDs and CDs show quasi-spherical morphology with size distribution of 3.5 ± 0.5 nm and 2.5 ± 0.5 nm, respectively [36]. Fig. 1c is the TEM image of the MoS2/AIZS/fCDs composite, showing a typical lamellar structure of MoS2 nanosheets with embedded small dots. HRTEM images (Fig. 1d) of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs further show that the 0.33 nm spacing is attributed to the AIZS (311) plane [35], the 0.32 nm spacing to the MoS2 (101) plane [37], and the 0.21 nm spacing to the fCDS (100) plane [33]. It proved that MoS2, AIZS and fCDs were successfully combined in the ternary composite. To further demonstrate the structure, Fig. 1e-1i show the EDX overlap and elemental mapping profiles of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs, where all the elements (Mo, C, Fe and In) can be observed with uniform distribution, indicating the incorporation of fCDs, AIZS QDs and MoS2 in the composites. It can be seen from Fig. 1e that the distribution of C, Fe, and In are well overlapped, indicating that AIZS QDs and fCDs were combined together and evenly dispersed on MoS2 nanosheets.

XRD was used to further characterize the crystal structure of the MoS2/AIZS/fCDs composites. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, three apparent peaks are located at 27.4°, 46.4° and 54.4°, which correspond to the (331), (440) and (533) planes of cubic phase AgIn5S8 (JCPDS No. 25–1329), respectively [35,38]. For MoS2, the characteristic peaks at 14.39°, 32.80°, 33.62°, 39.65°, and 58.55° are corresponding to (002), (100), (101), (103), and (110) planes of hexagonal phase MoS2 (JCPDS No. 73–1508), respectively [34,39]. The amount of MoS2 was also regulated. When 9% of MoS2 was introduced, there was no characteristic XRD peak of MoS2 observed, because of the low amount. With the increase of MoS2, three characteristic XRD peak of MoS2 were observed on 13% MoS2/AIZS/fCDs. Raman spectroscopy was further used to study the structure of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the broad weak peak around 300–360 cm−1 is attributed the PL of AIZS [35]. Both MoS2 and MoS2/AIZS/fCDs show similar peaks at 376.6 cm−1 and 402.8 cm−1, which are attributed the E2g and A1g vibrational modes of MoS2, proving successful combination of MoS2 with AIZS. However, the characteristic peak of fCDs near 1335 and 1600 cm−1 was not observed, probably due to the low content of fCDs. The chemical elements and valence state of the composite were further studied by XPS. In Fig. 2c, the two peaks located at 445.0 and 452.5 eV are attributed to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, proving the existence of In3+. The peaks at 161.9 and 163.1 eV are attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, proving the existence S2− (Fig. 2d) [40]. In Fig. 2e, the peaks at 229.2 and 232.4 eV are assigned to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 [34]. Two strong characteristic peaks at 704.1 eV and 719.1 eV correspond to typical Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, signals in FcA (Fig. 2f) [41]. Meanwhile, the high-intensity peak of In 3d proved the existence of AIZS QDs. All the other XPS spectra of Ag and Zn are provided in Fig. S1 in the supporting information.

The used fCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of FcA and CDs, which maintained the basic morphology and lattice spacing of the CDs with slight aggregation, as shown in the TEM and HRTEM images in Fig. S6. The Raman spectrum of fCDs (Fig. S6b) shows two peaks at 1335 and 1600 cm−1 corresponding the D band of sp3 carbon in disordered graphite and the G band of sp2 carbon in-plane vibration. AIZS/fCDs and AIZS/MoS2 were characterized in the supporting information. Fig. S4a & b are the TEM images, from which we can see the clear MoS2 nanosheets and the composite of FcA and AIZS QDs. In Fig. S4c&d, it can be distinguished that 0.27 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of MoS2, and 0.32 nm and 0.21 nm corresponds to the (310) plane of AIZS and the (100) plane of CDs. Fig. S5a shows the XRD patterns of AIZS/fCDs and AIZS/MoS2, in which the weak peaks at 26.3° and 32.8° correspond to the (002) plane of CDs and (100) plane of MoS2 (JCPDS No. 73–1508), respectively, and the three apparent peaks located at 27.4°, 46.4° and 54.4° correspond to the (331), (440) and (533) planes of cubic phase AgIn5S8 (JCPDS No. 25–1329). For FTIR, the peaks at 1389 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 correspond to O–H and C = O for L-cysteine on the QDs, and the peaks at 490 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 in Fig. S5b correspond to the characteristic peaks of C-Fe and C–C bonds of FcA in fCDs. Through Raman spectroscopy, it was found that A1g vibrational modes of MoS2 was observed at 402.8 cm−1 in AIZS/MoS2 (Fig. S5c).

Suitable energy level positions and band alignment play a key role in photocatalysis. In Fig. 3 a, AIZS QDs show an absorption edge of ∼ 660 nm. Compared to pure AIZS, AIZS/MoS2 and AIZS/fCDs show increase absorption, while the ternary MoS2/AIZS/fCDs nanocomposite show the highest light-harvesting capability, due to the successful introduction of MoS2 and fCDs. The band gap of AIZS QDs was estimated to be 2.06 eV (Fig. 3b). The valence band (VB) position of AIZS QDs was shown to be 1.09 eV in Fig. 3c. CB was then calculated to be −0.97 eV by ECB = EVB-Eg. According to previous reports, the energy band position of MoS2 [42] and fCDs[32] were adopted directly and the band alignment of the system is shown in Fig. 3d. Therefore, the photogenerated electrons will quickly transfer to the CB of MoS2 to participate the hydrogen revolution reaction, and then photogenerated holes in AIZS QDs can be transferred to the HOMO of fCDs. The MoS2/AIZS/fCDs ternary composites show a suitable band alignment for hydrogen production and potentially effective electron and hole transfer.

Charge transfer and recombination in the photocatalysts play a critical role on understanding the mechanism of catalytic process [26,43,44]. TPV spectroscopy was used to further study the interfacial charge transfer kinetics, such as the maximum charge extraction time, number of extracted electrons and interfacial charge lifetime, which allows more comprehensive understanding of the charge separation of the catalyst. However, TRPL can only reflect the charge separation in the interior of catalyst, which cannot directly reflect the charge separation and transfer to the surface because of the internal and external charge capturing.

TPV spectroscopy was used to further study the interfacial charge transfer kinetics of the composites[26,43]. First, TPV curves of pure AIZS (blue curve) and AIZS/fCDs (red curve) were measured to investigate the role of fCDs, as the hole acceptor, on effectively promoting the charge separation efficiency (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4a, the TPV relaxation curves show that the photovoltage intensity of AIZS/fCDs is much higher than AIZS. Next, the maximum charge extraction time (tmax) can be used to evaluate the electron extraction rate (or electron diffusion rate). However, Fig. 4b shows that AIZS/fCDs has a nearly unchanged electron extraction rate (tmax2, 0.0864 ms) compared to that of pure AIZS (tmax1, 0.089 ms). In Fig. 4c, the integrated area of the shaded part (A) represents the number of extracted electrons. A2 of AIZS/fCDs is 0.0147, which is 1.4 times greater than AIZS (A2, 0.0108), indicating that more photo-generated electrons were successfully transferred to the surface of AIZS/fCDs. As shown in Fig. 4d, the charge recombination process was further evaluated by the attenuation constant (τ). It shows that the charge recombination rate of AIZS/fCDs (τ2 = 0.1645 ms) is slower than AIZS (τ1 = 0.1442 ms). This shows that fCD, can effectively promote the hole extraction, so that more electrons are transferred to the surface and subsequently the occurrence of charge recombination was reduced slightly.

Next, we compared TPV results of AIZS QDs and AIZS/MoS2 (Fig. 5 ) to investigate the role of MoS2, on facilitating electron extraction. As shown in Fig. 5a, the TPV relaxation curves show that the photovoltage signal of AIZS/MoS2 is much higher than that of AIZS. Fig. 5b shows that AIZS/MoS2 (tmax3 = 0.086 ms) has almost the same electron transfer rate of AIZS (tmax1 = 0.089 ms). As shown in Fig. 5c, the charge extraction efficiency A3 of AIZS/MoS2 is 0.0194, which is much higher than the A1 (0.0108) of AIZS, indicating that more photo-generated electrons were extracted on the surface of AIZS/MoS2. As shown in Fig. 5d, the attenuation constants of AIZS/MoS2 and AIZS are τ3 = 0.1769 ms and τ1 = 0.1442 ms, respectively, proving the charge reduced recombination in AIZS/MoS2. The main reason is that the photogenerated electrons of AIZS were effectively transfered to MoS2 and thus inhibited the charge recombination. It worth noting that, different from the literature[45], here we prove that MoS2 does not accelerate the electron extraction rate, but only contribute on reducing the charge recombination rate.

Finally, we conducted TPV tests on MoS2/AIZS/fCDs to explore the overall effect of the coexisting MoS2 and fCDs on synergetic electron/hole extraction and charge recombination processes. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the TPV relaxation curves show that the photovoltage signal of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs (red curve) is 3.16 times higher than that of AIZS (red curve), much superior to that in AIZS/fCDs and AIZS/MoS2. Fig. 6b shows that MoS2/AIZS/fCDs (tmax4 = 0.081 ms) has a slightly faster electron transfer rate than pure QDs (tmax1 = 0.089 ms). More importantly, as shown in Fig. 6c, the A4 of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs is 0.0262, which is 2.5 times greater than the A1 of AIZS (A1, 0.0108). However, the attenuation constant τ4 of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs (0.1391 ms) is smaller than τ1 (0.1442 ms) of AIZS (Fig. 6d), e.g., the coexisting MoS2 + fCDs increased the charge recombination in contrast to the reduced charge recombination by single fCDs or MoS2 (Fig. 4d). The main reason can be ascribed to that large number of electrons and holes were extracted simultaneously onto the surface of the ternary composite with strong electrostatic interaction, which may undergo severe interfacial charge recombination between different catalyst particles and lead to an increase in the overall charge recombination [46,47]. This is also a strong evidence for the synergistic electron/hole extraction in MoS2/AIZS/fCDs. The charge transfer kinetics of different samples was also studied by TRPL (Fig. S7). The fitting results for TRPL are summarized in Table S1. The average PL lifetime (τave) of AIZS/fCDs (25.91 ns) was dramatically decreased compared to AIZS QDs (79.31 ns), which can be attributed to the rapid hole extraction by fCDs. The PL lifetime of AIZS/MoS2 was decreased to 55.24 ns due to electron capturing on the rich edge defects of MoS2, which resulted in the decreased PL lifetime along with PL quenching. Moreover, under the decoration of both MoS2 and fCDs, the electrons and holes of AIZS were quickly extracted onto MoS2 and fCDs simultaneously, which greatly reduced the PL lifetime of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs (8.33 ns). The observation in the TRPL of different samples is consistent with that of TPV spectra in Fig. 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity vs. interfacial dynamics parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic performance of the above photocatalysts was investigated and compared with the TPV data in detail to better understand the role of MoS2 and fCDs. Hydrogen production increased linearly with the increase of illumination time (Fig. 7 a). The H2 evolution yield of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs nanocomposite reached 42.6 mmol g−1 after 5 h. As displayed in Fig. 7b, AIZS QDs show a H2 generation rate of 0.482 mmol g−1h−1. After loading fCDs or MoS2, the optimal H2 production rate reaches 2.253 mmol g−1h−1 and 2.97 mmol g−1h−1, which were 4.67 and 6.16 times higher than pure AIZS, respectively. When both of fCDs and MoS2 were added together, the optimal H2 production rate of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs reached 8.52 mmol g−1h−1, dramatically higher than that of AIZS QDs (17.67 times), which indicates the synergistic effect of election/hole coextraction. Compared with related photocatalysts with different charge separation enhancing strategies (Table S2), the photocatalytic activity of our ternary MoS2/AIZS/fCDs is among the highest level, showing the great potential of synergistic electron/hole extraction for future work. Moreover, in the four photocatalytic reaction cycles, the hydrogen production rate of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs did not decrease significantly, and the XRD did not change, indicating its good stability (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. 7c, fCDs and MoS2 has little effect on the charge extraction rate as indicated by the similar tmax of AIZS (0.089 ms), AIZS/fCDs (0.0864 ms), AIZS/MoS2 (0.0860 ms) and MoS2/AIZS/fCDs (0.0811 ms). In contrast, the amount of extracted electrons (A) increased dramatically from A1 = 0.0108 (AIZS) to A2 = 0.0147 (AIZS/fCDs), A3 = 0.0194 (AIZS/MoS2) and A4 = 0.0262 (MoS2/AIZS/fCDs), owning to the synergistic electron-hole extraction effect. Fig. 7e shows the attenuation constants of different samples. Although modification with fCDs (τ2 = 0.1645 ms) or MoS2 (τ3 = 0.1769 ms) can both enhance τ in binary composites, τ4(0.1329 ms) of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs is much lower than those with only fCDs or MoS2 modification, which can be ascribed to the increased interface charge recombination opportunity upon coextraction of electrons and holes. The result also strongly indicates the synergistic effect of electron/hole extraction with both fCDs and MoS2. Although MoS2/AIZS/fCDs shows shorter surface charge carrier lifetime in TPV, holes extracted by fCDs can preferentially react with sacrificial agents, which may lead to higher catalytic activity[48]. To better understand the effect of dual cocatalysts, the number of the effective charges of the composite material (or the number of charges remaining on the surface after the charge extraction and recombination) was further calculated by the formula[44]:.
A
eff
=
A τ
t max

As shown in Fig. 7f, Aeff are 0.0174 for AIZS (Aeff1), 0.0279 for AIZS/fCDs (Aeff2), 0.0399 for AIZS/MoS2 (Aeff3) and 0.0429 for MoS2/AIZS/fCDs (Aeff4), where Aeff4 of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs is not dramatically improved compared to MoS2/AIZS, despite the 17.67 times higher catalytic activity. For this observation, we need to recall the fact that, in addition to the widely reported charge separation, the enhancement of catalytic activity also involves the surface reaction rate, reactant concentration, light intensity and so on [28,32]. Therefore, Aeff is not directly related to the enhancement of activity and thus improving the efficiency by only considering charge separation is not enough. Here, the activity improvement of ternary MoS2/AIZS/fCDs composites is more attributed to the addition of active sites and the rapid transformation of the extracted electrons, e.g., the synergy and compromise effect of the active sites and charge extraction. More discussion will be provided upon the catalytic reaction kinetics and charge extraction kinetics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity optimization of the composite photocatalysts and active site
ce:section.ce:para: To better demonstrate the synergy between charge extraction and surface reaction; different content of MoS2 was introduced to AIZS/fCDs for testing the photocatalytic activity, to show catalytic active site in the catalytic system is one of the main functions to improve the activity[49,50]. As shown in Fig. 8 a & 8b, the H2 production rate increased from 2.35 mmol g−1h−1 (0%) to 8.40 mmol g−1h−1 (9%), and then decreased to 5.77 mmol g−1h−1 (13%) with the increase of MoS2. The main reason is that MoS2 nanosheets act as electron acceptor and the active sites of H+ reduction, which is crucial for the continuous extraction and converse of the electrons. However, addition of excessive MoS2 will hinder the photon absorption of AIZS. To better explain the role of MoS2 as the active site, the electrocatalytic HER test was performed by liner scan voltammetry (LSV). As shown in Fig. 8c, the HER overpotential of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs (648 mV) at 10 mA cm−1 is lower than that of AIZS/fCDs (769 mV) and AIZS (843 mV). Also, as shown in Fig. 8d, Tafel slope of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs is 244 mV decade−1, which is better than AIZS/fCDs (267 mV decade−1) and AIZS (296 mV decade−1). These results indicate that MoS2 acts as the HER active site.

To better understand the charge extraction and utilization processes, here we further show an apparent kinetics analysis on the H2 evolution over MoS2/AIZS/fCDs in the present system[43,51]. Briefly, the main H2 evolution reaction,
2 H
+
+ 2
e
-
→
H 2
, should proceed via a series of complex elemental reactions, including charge generation charge recombination, proton reduction by electrons, and hole scarification by AA (proton generation). Although all reactions are involved in different catalysts, the contribution of these reactions varies in the cases of pure AIZS, AIZS/MoS2 (electron extraction), AIZS/fCDs (hole extraction) and MoS2/AIZS/fCDs (electron/hole coextraction), which will be discussed below. Fig. S3a shows the EIS of AIZS QDs, AIZS/fCDs, AIZS/MoS2 and MoS2/AIZS/fCDs. The impedance radius of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs is smaller than the radius of others, indicating that the coulomb interaction force on the charge carriers becomes weaker under the synergistic extraction of electron/holes. The above relationship is also explained by transient photocurrent responses (Fig. S8). With the introduction of fCDs and MoS2, the PL intensity of AIZS/MoS2 and MoS2/AIZS/fCDs were reduced by 47% and 93% compared with AIZS QDs (Fig. S3b). It suggests that the hole extraction ability of fCDs and the electron extraction ability of MoS2 can effectively improve charge separation in ternary MoS2/AIZS/fCDs.

At the same time, after the hole extraction of fCDs, both the numbers of electrons and protons were changed, indicating the dominant role of hole extraction in photocatalyst design.

As discussed above in the TPV data in Fig. 7, the simultaneous efficient electron/hole extraction would result in severe charge recombination as indicated by the decreased attenuation constant τ. This phenomenon was even more severe during TPV tests for Aeff because of the charge accumulation in the dry particles, which was dramatically relieved in the actual photocatalysis due the fast exhaustion of the electrons and holes by adsorbed protons and AA molecules, respectively. The strong interaction of the negatively and positively charged species with decreased τ in TPV tests (Fig. 7f). This gives a huge k8 in the tiny catalyst particles, as shown in Eq. (15). So, we can conclude that, if the hydrogen production rate has been promoted enough with MoS2 and the hole extraction rate has been promoted enough with fCDs, the diffusion of dHA in hole scarification become rate-limiting in our ternary composite.

)
2
is summarized in Fig. 9 a to better illustrated the variation in the cases of pure QDs, hole extraction (binary AIZS/fCDs) and electron/hole coextraction (ternary MoS2/AIZS/fCDs). For the first time, our results pointed out a critical missing point for the use of different kinetics data by correlating them with the catalysis process, the comprehensive understanding of the mechanism and the accurate evaluation of catalyst design, especially the interaction and synergy of different strategies. Through the above analysis, we get the synergy mechanism diagram of MoS2/AIZS/fCDs. (Fig. 9b). The photogenerated electrons and holes in AIZS QDs are transferred to MoS2 and fCDs, respectively. Most of the electrons are transferred to the active sites of hydrogen evolution on MoS2 to produce hydrogen, which effectively promotes the enhancement of activity. The improved charge extraction is mainly due to the rapid extraction of holes in AIZS QDs by fCDs to reduce the occurrence of electron-hole recombination. CDs were used as hole transfer intermediates due to their larger contact area and multiple energy level, which can efficiently extract photo-generated holes in AIZS QDs and transfer them to FcA for further consumption. A part of the electrons cannot participate in the reaction in time, which recombine with the holes on other ternary catalysts. Among them, the improvement of catalytic activity mainly comes from the cooperation of synergistic extraction and active sites. Our results suggested the critical role of the surface reaction for electron conversion beyond the commonly mentioned charge extraction and separation model in photocatalysis borrowed from semiconductor physics.

Photocatalytic activity depends on the factors of charge separation, interface reaction rate, reactant concentration, light intensity and so on. For hydrogen production, it is generally believed that the HER mechanism of reducing protons to hydrogen under acidic conditions includes two possible reaction pathways: Volmer-Heyrovsky or Volmer-Tafel processes, which have been widely discussed in electrochemical reduction of protons to H2 in the solution. As the first step, the Volmer reaction refers to the initial adsorption of protons from an acidic solution (H++ catalyst → H*). Then the adsorbed H* will react with the protons in the water layer to form H2 (H* + H+ → H2), which is called Heyrovsky reaction. Alternatively, the combination of two H* atoms can also generate H2 molecules (H* + H* → H2), which is called the Tafel process. The reaction should proceed through a series of complex elemental reactions, as simplified in equations (1) to (4)SPS:refid::e1_e2_e3_e4_e5_e6_e7_e8_e9_e10_e11_e12_e13_e14. In the case of electron and hole extraction in our composites, the H2 precipitation reaction should proceed through a series of more complex element reactions, including the electron extraction, hole extraction and the dramatically enhanced electron-hole recombination as shown in (1) to (5). More importantly, when considering the effect of protons produced by the hole-based half-reaction, the hydrogen evolution reaction should also consider the elemental reactions of proton generation from the hole and AA, as in equations (1) and (6)SPS:refid::e1_e4. Based on the detailed discussion of H2 vs. Aeff, it can be seen that the hydrogen evolution reaction on the composites proceeds via the Volmer-Tafel process, e.g., involving the combination of two H* atoms to H2 (H* + H* → H2), which is actually a more favorable process in terms of reaction activity [51,52]. It is more and more realized that hole extraction is more important than electron extraction, but there is still no clear evidence of the kinetics. Along this line, it is a great breakthrough that we carried out this quasi-quantitative apparent kinetics discussion by correlating the surface charge transfer with the electrocatalytic HER process, which provides a critical guiding point on the catalyst design and activity promotion in the future.

In summary, we prepared a series of ternary MoS2/AIZS/fCDs composites by simple in-situ synthetic method. After optimizing, the best hydrogen production photocatalytic performance is 17.67 times that of AIZS QDs, and is also much higher than AIZS/MoS2 (2.86 times) and AIZS/fCDs (3.7 times), indicating the synergistic effect of electron/hole extraction. TPV was used to investigate the electron/hole extraction and recombination processes, which suggests the key role of the interaction and synergy of different strategies. For the first time, our results showed the hydrogen evolution reaction on the MoS2/AIZS/fCDs composites proceeds via the electrocatalysis based Volmer-Tafel process from the correlation of apparent kinetics analysis and charge extraction, a critical missing point in photocatalysis study.
